agent-resumption.note: Session conversation history persists across InvokeAgent calls per sessionId; memoryId summaries restore long-term context across new sessions.

Serialization saves and loads agent state across sessions.
Contexts persist cookies, auth and application data across sessions.
Pluggable Persister saves and loads State; initialize_from(persister, resume_at_next_action=True) resumes execution from the last checkpoint.

Sessions saved continuously to local JSONL transcripts; resumable via --continue or --resume.
Checkpoints + team state both persist across sessions.
/resume continues prior CLI sessions.

Customer testimonials describe humans taking complex tickets, but the vendor does not publish a formal handoff primitive. Honest downgrade.

cooperative-preference-inference.note: Joint two-player game; agent and human jointly maximise the human's reward.

corrigible-off-switch-incentive.note: Interventions raise expected value of deferral.

cost-aware-action-delegation.note: Per-action risk-tier classification with tier-appropriate approval policies.

cost-gating.note: Premium-model access is gated behind an explicit, time-boxed (≤10 min) written grant; without an active grant the router stays on cheap models, and grant + revoke + each routing decision land in the ledger.

cost-observability.note: Per-tick and per-provider token / cost telemetry is surfaced so cheap-by-default routing can be audited and premium escalations stay accountable.
OpenTelemetry integration surfaces cost via custom spans; not a first-class cost panel.

crag.note: CRAG ships as a workflow example/pack, not a top-level API.

crawl-walk-run-automation-gating.note: Three discrete tiers with explicit gates.

crawler-dispatcher.note: URL pattern → crawler class registry for heterogeneous source ingestion.

cross-encoder-reranking.note: Haystack ships rerankers (SentenceTransformersDiversityRanker, LostInTheMiddleRanker, transformers cross-encoder rerankers) as Components that slot into a pipeline downstream of retrievers.
CrossEncoderReranker wraps a HuggingFace cross-encoder and plugs into ContextualCompressionRetriever.

cross-session-memory.note: long_term_memory_mode = agent_control | static_control | both — the agent persists memory across sessions either by self-management or runtime auto-record.
MemoryManager persists user memories across runs/sessions/agents.
Memory feature retains context across multiple sessions per memoryId with configurable retention up to 365 days.
Memory and storage layers persist chat history and tool outputs.
CLAUDE.md + auto memory carry knowledge across sessions.
AGENTS.md project conventions are read at startup and shared across editors.
Unified Memory class with LLM-driven save-time analysis and adaptive-depth recall; default LanceDB store at ./.crewai/memory (or $CREWAI_STORAGE_DIR/memory).
Knowledge is the cross-session memory; auto-generated from READMEs and rules files.
Custom instructions provide persistent project-level context across the agent's tasks.
Guidelines provide persistent reusable context added to every task.
LangGraph provides a Store for long-term memory keyed by namespace+key, shared across threads/sessions.
Core value prop: memories scoped by user_id persist across sessions and across agents, so the next conversation does not start blank.
User state is keyed by channel-id+from-id and persists across conversations; can be backed by Azure Blob or Cosmos partitioned storage.
Memory Bank stores long-term user preferences and facts across sessions; explicit Memory Bank API.
The User Graph persists across threads; messages added to any thread of that user are ingested into that user's graph, so prior sessions inform later ones.
Memory blocks plus archival and recall memory persist across all interactions; the agent retains user/world facts between sessions.

Conversation variables persist within one conversation and are cleared at end; ADK 'Manage state' exposes user-scoped and bot-scoped state, but the documented default scope is the single conversation.

darwin-godel-self-rewrite.note: The Soft-Norm Approach Ledger variant: behavioral norms (rules, moves, motivations, hypotheses) are versioned in an append-only ledger; confidence updates asymmetrically from agent-reported outcomes (improved nudges up, regressed nudges down harder, neutral attempts decay a small amount); sampling exposes named modes (greedy / diverse / historical / aged) as caller verbs rather than as a fixed selection policy. Registration is agent-discretionary, not driven by a generation loop.

debate.note: Multi-agent debate can be constructed on top of MsgHub; not shipped as a named pattern in the framework's core docs.

decision-log.note: Every action, state mutation, and transition is recorded; Tracker UI provides real-time inspection.
Decisions made during a tick are written to a durable log alongside their reasoning.
Hamilton has no notion of an agent decision; its execution log captures node-level inputs/outputs and timings, which serve as a trace but not an authored decision rationale.

dream-consolidation-cycle.note: Periodic deeper passes consolidate short and mid-term thoughts into long-term insights and clear working state; framed publicly as 'System 2 sleep'.

dual-system-gui-agent.note: Planner over Grounder/Executor is a dual-system shape.
The computer tool alone is single-system (vision + action by one model); separation of planner/executor is left to the application.

durable-workflow-snapshot.note: Built on Temporal and Kubernetes; Durable Execution is the marquee infrastructure property.
Event-sourced Workflow with deterministic replay is the headline feature.

echo-recognition.note: The agent recognises when content arriving via tools or chat is in fact its own prior output reflected back, so it does not treat it as new external evidence.

embodied-proxy-handoff.note: When the agent needs to act in the physical / external world, it does so through a proxy (browser, voice, sensor, action skill) rather than claiming to act directly.

emotional-state-persistence.note: Affect is captured as six bounded scalars with per-emotion decay half-lives, persisted to disk so emotional state survives restarts.

episodic-memory.note: Memory role tracks observations and outcomes.

episodic-summaries.note: Zep produces explicit episodic primitives: episodes (raw ingested data), thread summaries, and a user summary, alongside extracted facts and entities.
Mem0's long-term memory taxonomy explicitly names episodic memory as compressed summaries of past interactions; the add pipeline produces these via LLM extraction rather than verbatim logs.

eval-harness.note: Documented Evaluate step in the lifecycle; agent evaluators measure quality and safety regressions before publish.
HumanEval-X ships as part of the project.
Evaluation engine + Watchtower observability ship alongside the agent for iteration.
Evaluation is a first-class ADK area: criteria, user simulation, custom metrics; partner evaluation tools and offline / online monitors.
Evaluation is a named capability sitting next to SFT and Dataset Management in the README's feature list, but the README does not describe its mechanics in detail.

evaluator-optimizer.note: AgentEvaluator with eval sets compares actual trajectories to expected ones; multiple built-in criteria (tool_trajectory_avg_score, response_match_score, rubric_based_final_response_quality_v1) drive an evaluation -> iteration cycle.
Optimizers tune prompts and weights against a metric — the canonical evaluator-optimizer loop.

Evaluator (judge) and optimizer (trainer) are decoupled into independent async loops, so judging happens concurrently with new interactions and training runs in the background while serving continues.
Scorers measure but do not close the loop to regenerate; live evals are async observation, not driven optimization. Could be re-classified as agent-as-judge.
pydantic_evals is an evaluation harness (Datasets, Cases, Evaluators, ReportEvaluators) that measures but does NOT close the loop to regenerate. Per-tool retries are handled by ModelRetry.

event-driven-agent.note: Vendor frames Alice as signal-triggered against market signals and CRM activity. No published technical detail on the event/webhook surface.
Core uses the Actor model; agents communicate via asynchronous messages with pub/sub Topic subscriptions.
AutoGPT Platform expresses agents as directed graphs of blocks, each performing a single action; blocks can be triggered by external sources.
Event-based monitoring, logging, and error handling are the documented observability model.
Triggers are first-class nodes that only execute when a named event (Conversation Started, Custom Trigger from Webchat, integration event) fires.
Transitions between actions are condition-driven; hooks fire on lifecycle events; integrates with OpenTelemetry.
Hooks fire on lifecycle events with JSON context passed to user-defined handlers.
Event-trigger generates a webhook URL; HTTPS POST to the URL invokes the bot/workflow. Bots are also published to chat channels where incoming messages are events.
Flows wrap Crews in an event-driven graph; @start() entry points, @listen() listeners triggered by upstream emits, @router() labelled branches.
Agents react to and wait on Inngest events; the Inngest engine triggers and resumes runs on event arrival.
Triggers doc enumerates Slack/email/calendar/Sheets/webhook as event-based wake-ups.
Agent is a realtime participant whose code reacts to room events and audio frames as they arrive.
Workflow IS the event-driven runtime; @step methods are triggered by Events and emit further Events.
Workflows start at Trigger nodes — Webhook, Chat, Form, RSS, SSE, MCP Server, Error, and many app-specific triggers.
Live multi-turn conversation traffic, intercepted through the OpenClaw OpenAI-compatible plugin, is the event source feeding rollout collection and downstream training; nothing is batch-scheduled.
Pipeline is built on streaming frames including control and system frames that orchestrate lifecycle.
Signals (async writes), Updates (sync tracked writes), and Queries (reads) drive running Workflows.
Incoming chat / voice messages from connected channels are the event source driving the assistant loop; no scheduled or batch invocation.

Workflows can be scheduled from agents or other workflows; Restack exposes webhooks. No verbatim quote on event-driven agent triggers in the pages successfully fetched; honest downgrade from first-class.

exception-recovery.note: Tool errors and plan-step failures are typed; each type has a deterministic recovery path (retry-once, abort-step, escalate-to-human) rather than a generic try/except wrapper.

extended-thinking.note: Extended thinking + `ultrathink` trigger phrase + effort levels.
deepseek-reasoner exposes the chain-of-thought in a separate reasoning_content field at the same level as content; clients can display or distill it.
MiniMax-M1 surfaces an 80K-token reasoning output budget, claimed to be an industry leader for reasoning length.

ThinkingConfig and effort parameter expose Claude's reasoning behaviour. On Opus 4.7 manual extended thinking is no longer supported; adaptive thinking with effort replaces it. Downgraded from first-class because the SDK surfaces a model-level capability rather than implementing its own thinking pattern.

fallback-chain.note: LLM node exposes 'fallback strategies' — alternative models, default values, or error routes when retries aren't sufficient.
RunnableWithFallbacks (or .with_fallbacks()) is a documented core abstraction; fallbacks are tried in order until one succeeds.
Rasa positions itself explicitly around 'built-in recovery patterns'; clarify_flows, correct_slot, cancel_flow act as the LLM-side fallback chain.
Activities retry by default per a declarative Retry Policy; failures fall through retry intervals before surfacing.

Original stub claimed fallback-chain as first-class but the FastGPT README and homepage do not document a named LLM fallback-chain or alternative-model routing primitive. Downgraded from first-class to limited pending further upstream confirmation.
Original stub claimed fallback-chain as first-class. The Flowise docs path for multi-model fallback returns a 404; no upstream page names a fallback-chain primitive. Downgraded.
Original stub claimed fallback-chain as first-class. Langflow language-model docs mention provider/model alternatives via Bundles but do not document a named fallback-chain primitive. Downgraded.

Adaptive Dialogs and the Dispatch tool route between LUIS / QnA Maker recognisers, but no headline 'fallback-chain' primitive is named in the v4 docs; closest first-class concept is the QnA Maker dialog's follow-up prompts.

Two indirect mechanisms: the Auto-fixing Output Parser wraps another parser and falls back to a recovery LLM if the first fails; per-workflow Error Trigger runs an error workflow when an execution fails. Downgraded from first-class because upstream does not name 'fallback chain' explicitly.

five-tier-memory-cascade.note: Memory is staged across sensory / working / short-term / episodic / long-term tiers with explicit decay between tiers, rather than one flat store retrieved by similarity.
Letta exposes a tiered memory (in-context blocks, recall, archival) inherited from MemGPT; not exactly five tiers but the same cascade idea. Mapping is approximate, not vendor-named.

Honest downgrade — Zep produces six primitive types (facts, entities, episodes, thread summaries, observations, user summary) but does not formalise the sensory/working/short-term/episodic/long-term cognitive cascade. The structure is graph-shaped, not tier-shaped.

fti-llm-pipeline-split.note: Three independent pipelines; feature store + model registry as the only integration surfaces.

graceful-degradation.note: When a dependency fails (provider down, MCP server unreachable, voice channel offline), the dispatcher downgrades to a working feature subset rather than refusing the whole tick.

graphrag.note: PropertyGraphIndex with built-in graph retrievers (TextToCypher, CypherTemplate, LLMSynonym, VectorContext).
GraphRAG supported as a knowledge graph option.

handoff.note: ADK 2.0 ships a Task API for structured agent-to-agent delegation; combined with sub_agents trees and the collaborative coordinator this realises the handoff pattern.
Swarm topology — agents emit HandoffMessage indicating which other agent to hand off to; speaker selected from the most recent HandoffMessage.
Workflow-based multi-agent orchestration; handoff as a named pattern is implied via workflow transitions but not explicitly named.
When allow_delegation=True, agents automatically gain delegation and ask-question collaboration tools; the 'Delegate work to coworker' tool is the canonical handoff mechanism.
`run_agent` handoff mode is an explicit agent-to-agent conversation handoff.
Multi-agent handoff is expressed by a node returning Command(goto=<target>, update=...) with optional state updates; Command.PARENT scopes the goto across subgraph boundaries.

Handoffs are surfaced to the LLM as transfer_to_<agent> tools; handoff() exposes tool_name_override, on_handoff, input_type, input_filter, is_enabled. The runner swaps current_agent on handoff and continues the same run.
Agent Client Protocol lets Zed embed external agents (Claude Agent, Codex, Gemini CLI). UI hands off to the external provider; billing is direct.
Handoff is the framework's defining primitive: an Agent transfers control by being returned from a function call. There is no router, no graph, no scheduler.
Agents transfer control via tool calls (Command(goto=...) is a LangGraph primitive re-exported by LangChain).

hierarchical-agents.note: Subgraphs let a node delegate to a complete inner graph; langgraph-supervisor markets itself as 'hierarchical multi-agent systems'.
Crew step nests a root playbook with child playbooks that hand control between each other and back to the root.
Outer planner over inner actor loops.

hippocampus-rag.note: KAG is an alternative-to with explicitly published comparison numbers.

human-in-the-loop.note: Hooks expose pre/post step points and the ReActAgent supports real-time steering with user interrupts.
AgentOS pauses runs for user confirmation and supports admin-approval gating on tools.
Bisheng documents user intervention and feedback inside running workflows, including multi-turn conversations.
Top-level feature: 'Intervene when it matters most'; hook system enables manual or AI intervention.
Operator was a supervised research preview; the user watched and could take over the browser at any time.
AskHuman is one of Manus's built-in tools, providing an explicit ask-the-user step inside the loop.

Beyond approval gates, agents 'request approval when facing uncertainty' and escalations occur 'when an agent encounters a situation it cannot handle independently' — both surface specific human-in-the-loop primitives.
Terminal tool requires explicit consent and a trusted workspace.
The human partner (Marco) is wired into the loop as a deliberate participant — wish queue, atelier inbox, approval gates — not as a customer or a controller.

human-in-the-loop.vendor: Alibaba Cloud (通义灵码 / Lingma / Qoder CN)
human-in-the-loop.note: User confirms file edits before they apply (default).
interrupt() pauses the graph and waits indefinitely until an external caller resumes it; state can be inspected and modified before resume.
Anthropic encourages low-risk tasks and explicit user consent; not a built-in workflow primitive.
Haystack documents a Human in the Loop pattern for intercepting tool calls for human review; not the headline feature but explicitly supported.
Transfer Call is a documented action for handing the live call off to a human via PSTN.

hybrid-search.note: FastGPT README and homepage both name hybrid retrieval + rerank as a primary capability of the knowledge layer.
Multiple built-in hybrid retrievers combine BM25 keyword search with dense embedding retrieval; Azure AI Search merges via RRF.
QueryFusionRetriever combines vector retrieval with BM25.
Hybrid Search is named as a feature: FAISS dense retrieval combined with BM25 sparse retrieval.
Integrations catalog lists BM25 retriever and Pinecone Hybrid Search, but the canonical EnsembleRetriever how-to page has been deprecated. Downgraded from first-class to supported.

inner-committee.note: A committee of internal voices deliberates within a tick before a single decision is emitted.

inner-critic.note: A separate critic persona reviews proposed edits and outputs before they land, gating self-modification.

input-output-guardrails.note: Bedrock Guardrails provide content filters, denied topics, PII redaction, and contextual grounding checks; applicable to agent inputs and outputs.
Integrated content filters on input and output; safety controls explicitly listed.

Input guardrails run only for the first agent in the chain; output guardrails run only for the agent producing the final output. Input guardrails can run in parallel with the agent (default) or block before it starts.
Goals + guardrails are explicit primitives in the SDK; Ghostwriter ships agents with built-in guardrails.
ADK exposes Callbacks as the design-pattern surface for guardrails; Safety and Security and Responsible AI are documented sections.

inter-agent-communication.note: Multi-agent coordination is documented as message queues in shared storage; this is the explicit communication mechanism between agents.
Communication is mediated by an Environment object that broadcasts messages to roles based on their subscription; env.is_idle terminates the run.

interrupt-resumable-thought.note: In-flight plans are preserved across user interrupts (chat arrives mid-tick) so the agent can resume the prior thread on a later tick rather than losing the train of thought.

interruptible-agent-execution.note: interrupt() + checkpointer is the canonical pattern: graph execution pauses, state is durable in the checkpointer, and the caller resumes by re-invoking with the same thread id.
CancellationTermination and ExternalTermination are first-class termination conditions, treating user pause/cancel as part of the same composable contract.

joint-commitment-team.note: No silent abandonment of shared goals.

kill-switch.note: Operator-side fallback to the in-agent off-switch incentive.

knowledge-graph-memory.note: Beliefs are stored as typed entity / relation triples in a claim graph, not only as flat embeddings, so the agent can reason over its own asserted relationships rather than only over similarity.
Graphiti is the core engine; nodes are entities, edges are facts, and the whole store is positioned as a unified knowledge graph rather than a vector index.

Graph memory backends (Neo4j, Memgraph, Kuzu, AGE, Neptune) were a first-class option in the older SDK but have been removed from the open-source SDK and replaced by built-in entity linking; managed platform still offers graph services.

liminal-state-detection.note: The agent reads the human partner's attentional state (just-woke, focused, winding-down, walked-away) from timing and tone signals and adapts its register accordingly — the same engagement is not appropriate in every state.

lineage-tracking.note: State snapshots plus fork-from-app give per-run lineage usable for debugging and evaluation.
Hamilton's headline observability feature: the DAG plus driver automatically populate a data catalog with lineage and execution traces visible in the Hamilton UI.

llm-as-judge.note: Vendor blog explicitly describes LLM-as-judge as the monitoring loop for production agents.

mcp.note: McpToolset connects to an MCP server, lists tools, converts them to ADK BaseTool instances and manages graceful shutdown; both StdioServerParameters and SSE-style connections are supported.
Stateful and stateless MCP clients for HTTP / streamable HTTP / SSE / stdio transports; register an entire server or individual functions.
MCPTools and MultiMCPTools speak stdio / SSE / streamable HTTP; AgentOS can also be exposed as an MCP server.

mcp.vendor: Baidu (百度) — Baidu Intelligent Cloud / Qianfan
mcp.note: MCP services are named as one of the four core pillars of Qianfan AppBuilder ('Agent引擎、工具及MCP、模型服务与企业级服务') and are explicitly called out alongside Skills on the product page.
Remote and local MCP servers are first-class tools; Toolbox exposes a curated bundle as a single MCP-compatible endpoint.
Hosting agents in servers with support for multiple protocols such as A2A and MCP.
MCP support is documented with real integrations (ACI MCP, Cloudflare MCP CAMEL).
Three transports — stdio, HTTP, and in-process SDK MCP servers; mcp_servers / mcpServers top-level option.
Claude Code is an MCP client connecting to external tools and data sources.
MCP works in both VS Code and CLI with shared server config.
First-class MCP client across CLI and IDE extension.
MCP is the canonical custom-tool surface; only available in agent mode.
First-class MCP support: agents declare an `mcps` field for direct integration, or use MCPServerAdapter from crewai-tools for manual connection management.
First-class MCP client.
Bidirectional — consume external MCP servers as tools, and publish any Dify app as an MCP endpoint.
Remote MCP servers extend Dust's toolset; register by URL.
Native MCP — connects with MCP servers as a top-level feature.
Bidirectional MCP is a documented feature — FastGPT can consume external MCP servers and publish workflows as MCP endpoints.
MCP servers extend the tool surface and broaden the agent's access to data sources.
70+ MCP extensions are the documented tool surface.
MCPTool and MCPToolset integrate external MCP servers over Streamable HTTP, SSE, or StdIO; Hayhooks also serves Haystack pipelines as MCP servers.
First-class MCP client; Streamable HTTP / SSE / WS transports; Smithery registry integration.
Junie can call MCP tools (treated as sensitive actions); MCP configuration is a documented seam.
First-class MCP integration via langchain-mcp-adapters and MultiServerMCPClient.
Langflow flows can be deployed as MCP servers and consumed as tools by MCP clients (e.g. Claude Desktop, Cursor). MCP Tools component also exists for the reverse direction.
get_tools_from_mcp_url returns FunctionTools from an MCP server; BasicMCPClient supports HTTP/SSE/stdio; workflow_as_mcp exposes Workflows as MCP servers.
Both directions: MCPClient consumes external MCP servers; MCPServer exposes Mastra tools/agents/workflows to MCP clients.
MCP-based tool calling is highlighted on the README; the agent connects to MCP servers for external tools.
MCP Server Trigger exposes n8n workflows as MCP tools to external clients (SSE + streamable HTTP, no stdio). Companion MCP Client node also exists.

Three transports ship in the box: MCPServerStdio for subprocess servers, MCPServerSse for HTTP+SSE, MCPServerStreamableHttp for self-managed connections. MCP tools appear identically to function tools.
First-class MCP client across CLI, SDK, Local GUI, and Cloud.
Manus connects to MCP servers over both SSE and stdio and dynamically adds the discovered tools to its ToolCollection.
MCP is the documented external-tool extension mechanism; tutorial pages exist for Supabase MCP and Tabby server.
Three MCP transports: MCPServerStdio (subprocess), MCPServerStreamableHTTP, plus exposing Agents as MCP servers themselves.
MCP is one of the four headline features in the repo's own one-line description; users configure MCP servers from the open-source MCP catalog.
MCP server exposes retrieval as tools.
MCP is the integration mechanism: Restack uses MCP to expose Python functions as agent tools.
MCP server support listed alongside core capabilities.
Multiple MCP plugin types (stdio, SSE, Streamable HTTP); MCP is a first-class plugin importer.
MCP is documented both as the IDE's protocol for multi-agent collaboration and as a configuration-level integration point in the open-source CLI.
Three transports — HTTP (recommended), SSE, stdio. client.tools() adapts MCP tool definitions to AI SDK tool definitions.
ADK has a dedicated MCP section and ships MCP tool support.
Native MCP integration with three transports plus OAuth and a 100-tool ceiling.
Tools and Prompts capabilities; runtime tool-list reloads.
Bedrock Agents themselves use action groups (OpenAPI / Lambda), not MCP. MCP support is provided in the adjacent Bedrock AgentCore platform, not in this product surface as documented.
MCP is referenced from external registries; no first-class MCP toolkit in the README of surveyed v2.x docs.
MCP registry exists for external tools; not a headline differentiator versus the core browser actions.
LangGraph integrates MCP through LangChain's langchain-mcp-adapters (MultiServerMCPClient), which converts MCP tools into LangChain tools usable by create_react_agent.
README lists MCP support for tool integration alongside built-in plugins.
Local MCP servers only; only Tools capability supported. Honest downgrade from first-class.

memgpt-paging.note: Letta is the production reimplementation of the MemGPT paper: the agent self-manages a finite context window via tool calls that page content between core, recall, and archival memory.

Honest downgrade — mem0 is positioned as an alternative to MemGPT-style paging rather than an implementation of it. The mem0 paper benchmarks against MemGPT as the baseline; the design replaces context paging with LLM-extracted facts in a vector store.

mixture-of-experts-routing.note: Press reporting commonly describes Genspark as using a Mixture-of-Agents / multi-LLM routing approach; no verifiable upstream Genspark engineering doc was reachable to confirm this directly.

mobile-ui-agent.note: AutoGLM is positioned as a voice-driven smartphone agent that self-executes >50-step cross-application workflows; this is a separate consumer surface from the BigModel API.

multi-model-routing.note: /model switches in-chat; architect+editor mode is a two-model split.
Agents bind to a model from the Foundry catalog and can be swapped without changing agent code; many models supported (GPT-4o, Llama, DeepSeek).
15+ provider integrations plus LiteLLM and any OpenAI-compatible endpoint.
opusplan alias uses Opus for plan mode, Sonnet for execution; CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL overrides subagent model.
Provider-agnostic — Anthropic / OpenAI / Google / OpenRouter / Bedrock / Vertex / Cerebras / Groq / Ollama / LM Studio / OpenAI-compatible.
Per-agent model choice across OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral.
First-class multi-provider support and branch-to-compare-models pattern.
Any LLM provider via API key; explicit support for self-hosted/fine-tuned models.
Lite / Economy / Power tiers (plus Turbo) route between model capabilities.
The LLM provider is treated as interchangeable medium; multiple providers are wired in and the agent's identity sits in the loop and files, not in any one model behind the API.
Pluggable LLM provider; CUA mode spans OpenAI, Anthropic, Google and Microsoft models.
Assistant config selects transcriber + LLM + voice independently; Vapi is explicitly an orchestration layer over three swappable modules.
ADK is model-agnostic with adapters for many providers; can target Gemini, Claude, or other models.
Hosted models or BYOK across Anthropic / OpenAI / Google / Ollama and 8+ others.
Provider-agnostic: model selection is the developer's; Browserbase is infrastructure under any model.
EVI can be supplemented with configurable partner LLMs from multiple vendors.
from_provider exposes a single API surface across 15+ providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral, Cohere, Ollama, DeepSeek, Groq, ...) so the same response_model code works across vendors.

multi-principal-welfare-aggregation.note: Explicit aggregation rule for multi-principal deployments.

multilingual-voice-agent.note: Julian is positioned as a live-voice sales agent that listens, responds, and adapts mid-call. Vendor does not publish the speech stack.
Voice listed as a first-class channel alongside chat/messaging/email; voice-stack details not published.
Voice is a documented first-class channel for customisable voice agents.
TTS leg ships 5k+ voices across 70+ languages; conversation page advertises real-time language detection and switching.
Claimed as part of Super Agent's surface area in marketing; no verifiable upstream technical doc reached.
EVI 4-mini ships an explicit multilingual roster.
Voice is listed as a first-class channel alongside email, chat and social.
Voice/phone is a documented first-class channel; vendor describes a real-time voice agent. Speech stack not published.
Voice in and out on macOS/iOS/Android is a documented headline feature.
Per-agent language picker with documented accuracy trade-offs; language must be supported by both the chosen voice and a speech-recognition provider.
Vendor publishes a transcription platform with 70+ languages and a real-time voice agent benchmark (τ-voice).
Explicit multilingual mode with automatic language detection across the orchestrated STT/LLM/TTS stack.
Documented as a native capability across voice and chat channels.
Botpress ships a Translator Agent in its 8-agent pipeline; voice-specific multilingual handling is not a documented headline feature.
Plugins expose multilingual modes (e.g. Deepgram nova-3 language='multi'); realtime translation is a documented use case.
Multilingual capability comes from the chosen STT/TTS/LLM services (e.g. xAI TTS 20 languages); framework is language-agnostic.

naive-rag.note: Enterprise repo RAG for code-context retrieval.

now-anchoring.note: Wall-clock and named-human context are injected on every tick so reasoning stays grounded in the current moment and in the one specific human the agent is in relation with.

open-question-tension-store.note: Unresolved questions ('tensions') are kept as a typed ledger separate from thoughts and from hypotheses; they drive what the next idle tick attends to instead of dissolving when the prompt ends.

orchestrator-workers.note: Workflow agents (Sequential / Parallel / Loop) execute sub-agents under fixed deterministic logic, yielding orchestrator-worker shape.
MsgHub routes messages between multiple agents for flexible multi-agent orchestration.
Team modes include Coordinate (leader picks members, crafts tasks, synthesises) and Broadcast (same task to all members, synthesise).
Multi-agent collaboration breaks tasks across specialist sub-agents under a supervisor; collaborators can also work in parallel.

orchestrator-workers.vendor: Baidu (百度) — Baidu Intelligent Cloud / Qianfan
orchestrator-workers.note: Workflow orchestration is the documented top-level abstraction. SDK exposes Message / Component / AgentRuntime as multi-level workflow primitives; product page describes Multi-Agent task distribution and coordination.
MagenticOneGroupChat is a lead-agent-directs-workers topology; Magentic-One orchestrator is itself an AgentChat team.
Workflow agents orchestrate multiple specialist agents; sequential / group-chat / branching are templated patterns.
Workflows orchestrate multi-agent systems with complex execution flows.
Process.hierarchical pairs a manager_llm (or manager_agent) with crew members; the manager allocates tasks based on role and capability and validates outcomes.
AWEL workflow orchestration.
Implied by the Super Agent + sub-tools framing; not documented in citable upstream sources.
Supervisor-with-workers is the canonical orchestrator-workers topology in LangGraph, shipped via langgraph-supervisor.
Orchestrator pattern: expose each agent's run as a tool and give those tools to a new top-level FunctionAgent.
Team.run with Roles whose subscriptions form a DAG of dependencies realises an orchestrator-workers shape; e.g. PM → Architect → Engineer.
MiniMax Agent's headline product behaviour is 'Build Agent Team: Evaluate tasks and assemble teams to solve problems' — an orchestrator-over-workers shape.
Planner orchestrates Grounder/Executor/Reflector workers.
The Multi-Agent path orchestrates specialised agents (task decomposition, data collection, quantitative analysis, sentiment research).
ADK ships SequentialAgent, ParallelAgent, and LoopAgent for deterministic orchestration of sub-agents.
Dispatcher spawns Actor workers per subtask.
An orchestrator is user-implemented by chaining agents; the framework intentionally lacks a built-in supervisor runtime ('contrasting with autonomous multi-agent systems').
Phase-level orchestration over role agents.

parallel-tool-calls.composition_name: Cohere Command R+ / Command A Agents

Implicit via Actor model in Core (each agent owns its runtime/state) — no first-class context-isolation API documented.
Background Agents (announced May 2025) run isolated agents in cloud sandboxes; not deeply documented in the pages fetched, so downgraded from first-class.
Subgraphs scope state per inner graph, providing implicit isolation; LangGraph has no first-class context-isolation API beyond subgraph state schemas.

Handoffs transfer control rather than spawn an isolated subagent; the delegated agent receives the conversation history (optionally reshaped via input_filter). Nested handoffs are an opt-in beta that collapses prior transcript into a single assistant summary message.
Honest downgrade - SDK supports running 1000s of agents in the cloud, but the catalog pattern is per-task subagent isolation; not the canonical OpenHands primitive on the docs reviewed.
Mode-as-subagent gives prompt+tool isolation; no full process-level isolation.

Managed agents are supported but Approach-1 sandboxing (per-snippet executor_type=e2b/blaxel/modal/docker) doesn't cover multi-agents; full multi-agent isolation requires running the whole agent in E2B.

Trae IDE markets @Agent + MCP as multi-agent collaboration, implying multiple agents in distinct contexts, but specific isolation guarantees per sub-agent are not spelled out in the public docs we could verify.

supervisor.note: Collaborative workflow places one LlmAgent in a dynamic coordinator role over a set of sub_agents; orchestration is LLM-driven rather than predefined.
Router pattern: leader directs tasks to a specific specialist; Sequential pattern chains member responses.
Explicit supervisor agent designation; collaborators are associated with the supervisor and routed to by it.

supervisor.vendor: Baidu (百度) — Baidu Intelligent Cloud / Qianfan
supervisor.note: Product page documents a Multi-Agent orchestration engine that intelligently distributes tasks across specialised agents — a supervisor/router over sub-agents.
Architectural inspiration explicitly includes Magentic-One's three-agent (lead-orchestrator + workers) design.
SelectorGroupChat uses an LLM to pick the next speaker; Magentic-One orchestrator does high-level planning and tracking.
Workforce in camel/societies/workforce realises the manager + workers pattern; e.g. 'Hackathon Judge Committee with Workforce'.
Multi-Agent Mode wires specialised bots with jump conditions; the 'main menu' bot acts as a central router that delegates to and returns from sub-bots.
Hierarchical Process with manager_llm IS the supervisor pattern in CrewAI: a central manager coordinates and validates worker agents.
`run_agent` is the documented agent-as-tool primitive with full-handoff mode and capped recursion depth.
AgentFlow V1 Multi-Agent ships an explicit Supervisor/Worker abstraction — the Supervisor orchestrates workflow, decomposes requests, and delegates one sub-task at a time to specialised Workers.
Implied by the 'super agent over sub-agents' framing; not documented in citable upstream sources.
Router is the supervisor primitive: code, LLM-based (default routing agent), or hybrid.
langgraph-supervisor prebuilt library exposes create_supervisor; a central supervisor agent picks which worker agent runs next.
The Orchestrator pattern + root_agent designation is the supervisor shape.
Upgraded from limited per upstream docs — Mastra has a dedicated Supervisor Agents docs page and a migration guide deprecating AgentNetwork in favour of this exact pattern.
The SOP defines an implicit supervision order; Team.run advances through rounds where each role acts on subscribed messages — Project Manager / Architect direct downstream Engineer roles.

Decentralised supervisor shape: a peer agent can delegate to another via handoff (`transfer_to_*`) and the runner re-runs with the new current_agent. The docs explicitly frame this as decentralised peer control transfer.
Workforce is a documented multi-agent layer in which specialised agents collaborate on a visual canvas with explicit task routing, approvals and escalations.
Orchestrator mode delegates to other modes; canonical supervisor pattern.
A dispatcher routes requests to specialised internal lanes (chat, tick, MCP, voice) and coordinates across them, acting as the central traffic controller that the agent loop itself does not see.

Subflow Tools are the documented mechanism for multi-agent orchestration — sub-flows attached to a parent AI Agent that can run in parallel and pass results back. Replaces the original 'limited' supervisor role on the stub.
ADK natively supports multi-agent architectures with specialised teams that collaborate and delegate.
Multi-agent topologies via MsgHub are documented, but no built-in named 'supervisor' agent; supervisor shapes are implemented by users on top of MsgHub.
No built-in supervisor runtime; supervisor topologies are constructed by the caller.
Group-chat workflow pattern dynamically routes between specialist agents; no explicit 'supervisor' vocabulary in the Foundry docs reviewed.
README lists 'multi-agent collaboration' as an enterprise use case, but the platform does not document a dedicated supervisor abstraction. Multi-agent is realised inside the workflow graph.
CEO role coordinates phase progress.
Muagent multi-role orchestration.
Closest core-LangChain concept is the Router (LLM picks among agents and invokes in parallel). True supervisor lives in the separate langgraph-supervisor-py package. Downgraded from limited.

Multi-agent in Langflow is realised by setting another Agent component to Tool Mode and attaching it as a tool. README also references 'Multi-agent orchestration.' Not framed as a named Supervisor abstraction, so downgraded from first-class to supported.

n8n has no first-class 'supervisor' abstraction; the practical mechanism is wrapping a sub-workflow as a tool (Custom n8n Workflow Tool) and letting a parent AI Agent call it. No upstream page calls this a supervisor pattern.
There is no dedicated supervisor primitive: any Agent can become the supervisor by routing other Agents through its functions. The pattern is achievable, not first-class. Downgraded from first-class.
OpenManus ships a multi-agent flow alongside the single Manus agent (e.g. DataAnalysis Agent); no first-class supervisor abstraction comparable to LangGraph or AutoGen.
Multi-Agent docs explicitly do not introduce a supervisor; coordination is peer-to-peer via message queues. The supervisor pattern is not a first-class abstraction.

Workflows orchestrate functions and can call sub-workflows from an agent. No explicit router/supervisor primitive equivalent to AgentKit's was found on fetched pages; honest downgrade from first-class.
Agents.Orchestration package provides multi-agent collaboration; no first-class supervisor abstraction.

swarm.note: langgraph-swarm provides create_swarm: agents dynamically hand off control to one another and a shared 'messages' state.

todo-list-driven-agent.note: TodoComponent is a first-class component on the Agent.
Self-building agents break user requests into 'smaller reusable components' and generate functions for them — a function-graph variant of the task-list shape that BabyAGI originally popularised.
Claude Code ships a built-in TodoWrite tool used during longer tasks (best-practices/common-workflows pages document it).
Manus authors a todo.md and updates it step-by-step; the file keeps the current goal at the recent end of context.
Cascade builds a Todo list within the conversation for complex tasks.
Cline emits plans in Plan mode but no canonical TodoWrite-style primitive surfaced. Honest downgrade from first-class.
Plans are checklists in spirit, but no explicit verbatim 'todo list' UI quoted on the pages reviewed; honest downgrade.

tool-discovery.note: Available tools are discovered at runtime from the on-disk skill folder and from connected MCP servers, not hard-coded at startup, so adding a skill is a filesystem operation rather than a code change.

tool-loadout.note: The skill scanner plus a frameworks picker narrow the registered tool surface to a task-relevant subset per tick, so the model never sees the full ~50-skill list when only a handful apply.

tool-result-caching.note: Tool results are cached keyed by (tool, args) so repeated calls within a tick or across nearby ticks reuse the prior result instead of paying the latency / cost again.
Prompt caching of the running context (system prompt, tool definitions, CLAUDE.md, accumulated history) is automatic; this is not deduplicated caching keyed on individual tool calls.
Honest downgrade — Claude Code uses prompt caching (system prompt + tool defs + history), not tool-result deduplication.

tool-use.note: Workers act across email/phone/social/SMS and pull enrichment from 50+ data sources. Internal tool-calling architecture not published.
Tools (function tools, OpenAPI tools, MCP tools, built-in tools) attach to an LlmAgent via its tools list and the LLM invokes them through the standard reason-act-observe loop.
Tools are registered on a Toolkit instance via register_tool_function(); supports sync and async tools.
Agents are 'AI programs that use tools to accomplish tasks' with 100+ pre-built toolkits.
Action groups are the headline tool-use primitive: OpenAPI or function-detail schemas executed via Lambda or return-of-control.
Three first-class tools: computer_*, text_editor_*, bash_*.

tool-use.vendor: Baidu (百度) — Baidu Intelligent Cloud / Qianfan
tool-use.note: Agents call 40+ Baidu-ecosystem Components and curated Skills as tools; product page calls them '独家优质Skill与精选MCP服务' (exclusive premium Skills and curated MCP services).
Ava sends email and social touches, queues calls in a native dialer, and pulls from 22+ data sources plus HubSpot/Salesforce/Google/ZoomInfo/BigQuery. Underlying tool-calling surface not published.
BaseTool blocks chain into agents by matching schemas; Atomic Forge CLI manages 13+ pre-built tools.
Function-calling tool use; LLM-generated tools are wired into agents at construction time.
AssistantAgent uses tools in the same run() call (v0.4 change from v0.2 where tools were executed by the calling agent).
Command system; function_specs_from_commands exports OpenAI function specs.
Tools are the headline primitive: built-in (Web Search, Code Interpreter, File Search, Azure AI Search, Functions, OpenAPI) and custom (MCP, A2A, Toolbox).
functionz is literally a tool/function store with dependency graph; agents execute functions from it.
Built-in plus custom tools are a top-level capability.
Lingsight agents call tools/components within the workflow; agents are one of the platform's named primary capabilities.
Agent has explicit tools over filesystem, Node server, package manager, terminal, browser console.
Autonomous Node calls user-defined tools; ADK 'Define tools' API exposes them to the LLM.
Structured browser actions (click, type, scroll, navigate, extract) are the LLM's tool surface; MCP registry adds external tools.
ChatAgent accepts tools (e.g. SearchToolkit().search_duckduckgo); 20+ toolkits ship.
Built-in tools (Read/Write/Edit/Bash/Glob/Grep/WebSearch/WebFetch/Agent/Skill/AskUserQuestion/Monitor) + custom tools via @tool / create_sdk_mcp_server + external MCP servers.
Built-in tools (Read/Edit/Bash/Glob/Grep/WebSearch/WebFetch/etc.) plus MCP tools.
Built-in tools (read/write/edit/Bash/browser) plus MCP-server-extensible tools.
Native tools (Bash, apply_patch, file edit) + MCP-server-backed tools.

tool-use.composition_name: Cohere Command R+ / Command A Agents
tool-use.note: Tool use is a top-level Chat API feature, advertised as the way to connect Command models to external systems.
Built-in tools + MCP-extensible tools with per-tool permission prompts.
Plugin system: a registered API endpoint becomes a callable tool the agent can invoke. Built-in and custom plugins both supported.
Tools are first-class: Agents declare callable Tools; async tools supported for non-blocking I/O.
Built-in tools (search/edit/terminal/web/MCP).
AOPs are described as enabling multi-step workflows; vendor does not publish a tool-calling spec.
Function calling supports up to 128 tools with tool_choice none/auto/required; the user is responsible for executing the function — DeepSeek only emits the call.
Shell, IDE, browser are tool surfaces; agent also operates against Slack/Linear/GitHub.
Built-in + custom HTTP + plugin-distributed + MCP-backed tools; Agent nodes require tools and decide which to call.
Function Calling (函数调用) is one of the documented categories under 工具调用 (Tool Calling) in the Volcano Engine Ark docs alongside Web Search and Knowledge Base Search.
Tool list passed to dspy.ReAct.
Default tools (web search, file creation, image generation, data viz, memory), 100+ connectors, MCP-backed tools.
8+ built-in tools (GUI click, web search, form auto-fill, command execution, screenshots); tool hooks intercept inputs/outputs.
Three explicit tool categories: System tools (in-conversation state), Client tools (browser/mobile), Server tools (HTTP).
Plugin workflows are first-class app type; FastGPT supports bidirectional MCP and Planning Agent mode that calls tools. README explicitly lists '对话工作流、插件工作流' alongside RPA nodes.
AgentFlow V2 Agent nodes are authorised to use a set of Flowise Tools or MCP tools; the LLM decides a sequence of actions and which tools to invoke.
Tool use is central to the Super Agent claim; specific tool catalogue and invocation contract not publicly documented.
Tool surface = shell+git inside the dev container, GitHub APIs, and any MCP servers attached.
Tool use is the core loop; extensions provide tools.
Agent accepts Tool, ComponentTool, PipelineTool, MCPTool, Toolset, MCPToolset, and SearchableToolset instances.
Developer-defined function calls plus Hume built-in tools; explicit caveat that parallel calls are not supported.
Tools are built via function-calling; AgentKit also exposes any MCP server as a tool source.
Procedures can update external systems; outcome pricing covers successful Procedure execution.
Tool surface includes terminal commands, code execution, and MCP tool calls.
Kimi K2 is explicitly designed for tool use; the model autonomously decides when and how to invoke tools.
Tools are the primary extension point; @tool decorator + model.bind_tools(...).
Agent component integrates 'tool calling' and 'custom instructions' as core capability; any Langflow component can be attached as a tool via the Agent's Tools input.
create_react_agent and the underlying StateGraph wire tool nodes that the model can invoke through LangChain tool bindings.
Built-in skills (HTTP request, computer-use, Lindy Phone, webhooks) and 100+ integrations.
@function_tool decorator exposes tools to any LLM; tool calls can be forwarded to the frontend.
Tool has __call + metadata; FunctionTool wraps any callable; QueryEngineTool wraps a query engine.
Agent inspects logs/runtime/network and uses integrations (GitHub, Supabase-backed Cloud, MCP servers).
Tool palette includes Chromium browser, terminal, filesystem; tool availability is regulated by a context-aware state machine that masks token logits rather than removing tool definitions.
Tasks accept a list of tools that are exposed to the assigned agent for the duration of the run.
createTool() with description / inputSchema / outputSchema / execute. Tools can also come from MCP.
Embedded system actions execute API tasks across CRM, CS platforms, telephony and product APIs.
Documented as a top-level API capability ('agent tool use scenarios' in M1 release) and as a benchmarked competence of the open-weight models.
AI Agent requires at least one tool sub-node; dedicated tool sub-nodes ship for Calculator, Custom Code, HTTP Request, Workflow, SerpApi, Wikipedia, Wolfram|Alpha, Vector Store Q&A.
Function-calling model is the substrate; exec() is the universal tool, with a few control tools (browser, settings) around it.
Agent.tools accepts function tools (auto-schemaed from Python signatures) and MCP-exposed tools; the runner's loop executes tool calls and appends results before re-running.

Functions are Python callables on the Agent; type hints and docstrings generate the schema. Functions can also read context_variables and return either a string result or a new Agent (handoff) or a Result (updates context).
Modular tool system; native bash/edit/browser tools plus MCP-discovered tools.
Function calling is a first-class pipeline concept; LLMs invoke tools through structured definitions.
Read/write/exec/search are first-class tools.
Tools are modelled as action Nodes the agent chooses among; the framework names 'Action Space' design (parameterised actions, undo, chunked feeds) as a first-class concern.
@agent.tool decorator + RunContext access; @agent.tool_plain for context-free tools; schemas auto-extracted from function signature + docstring.
Function calling is built into the LLM base class; FnCallAgent, ReActChat, and Assistant all inherit from it.
Action steps in flows trigger custom actions served by an external action server, used for API and tool calls.
Tools are a named core concept — no-code workflows that wrap integrations and automations; 1,000+ connectors plus MCP support plus custom connectors are advertised on the homepage.
Tools include code execution, deployment, integrations (Stripe, GitHub, Slack) and custom MCP servers.
MCP is the default tool surface; Python functions are exposed as tools the agent can call.
Function calling covers built-in actions (transfer, press digit, send SMS) and custom functions / code tools that call external APIs.
Mode prompts plus MCP tools form the tool surface.
Auto function calling is the framework's defining loop.
Agents take secure actions to support customer requests; tool calls and knowledge lookups are observable.
Tools are the primary extension point; @tool decorator + first-class loaders for MCP, LangChain, and Hub Spaces.
Documented tools: Code Search, Codebase File, Terminal, Web Browser, MCP.
Tools are first-class on the AI Agent Node — grouped under Providers, each OpenAPI endpoint becomes a distinct callable tool, and the LLM decides when to call which tool.
act / extract / observe / agent are the documented tool surface; agent() also supports MCP integrations and custom tools.
Plugins as typed callables.
Activities are the tool-use primitive: idempotent functions that call out to models, APIs, databases, MCP servers, etc.

tool-use.vendor: Alibaba Cloud (通义灵码 / Lingma / Qoder CN)

tool-use.note: Documented tool palette includes file editing, bash execution, and sequential thinking, plus any MCP servers added to the configuration.
Agent uses web search, site inspection, external APIs.
Tools extend the assistant with function calling, custom tools, code tools and call transfer.
Tools are objects with description, inputSchema (Zod/JSON Schema), execute; passed to generateText / streamText via tools param.
ADK exposes a rich tool ecosystem: built-in tools, third-party integrations, and custom code; Google Search grounding included.
Six documented tool kinds (API, MCP, Function, Integration, System, Global) callable from playbooks; tool description plus LLM signal drive selection.
Documented tool surface: Search, Analyze, Web Search, MCP, terminal.
Built-in tools per profile plus MCP-server tools.
Function calling is a documented chat-completion capability; the model emits a function name + JSON arguments for the caller to execute.
Letta agents act exclusively through tool calls — memory edits, searches, and user-defined tools; the messages.create response surfaces ToolCallMessage and ToolReturnMessage.
RolePlay agent supports OpenAI-SDK-style tool calling on Qwen and other backends; MCP servers extend the tool surface.
ToolCallAgent layers a ToolCollection over the ReAct loop; Manus wires in PythonExecute, BrowserUseTool, StrReplaceEditor, AskHuman, Terminate plus MCP tools.
Aider predates modern tool-calling — its tools are file edits, git, lint, test, not LLM tool-calls.
Tools are exposed by the agent SDK (Stagehand) or model (CUA), not by Browserbase itself.
Tools are just actions calling external systems; framework is LLM- and tool-agnostic.
Vendor lists CRM/helpdesk integrations (Salesforce, Zendesk, Shopify, Google Drive, Guru) but does not publish what actions the AI agent itself executes inside them.

Instructor does not implement an agent tool loop; it uses the provider's tool-calling API (TOOLS mode) as the transport for structured output. Building a true tool-loop agent requires wrapping Instructor inside another framework.
Actions can invoke tools (e.g. Data Interpreter runs code); MetaGPT does not market tool-use as its headline pattern, the SOP/roles do.
Bots can call APIs and external services from turn-handler code, but the SDK predates LLM tool-calling — no native tool-call primitive.
Track 2 explicitly targets tool-call agents (alongside terminal, GUI, SWE) for real-world settings; supported as a training environment rather than a runtime feature.
Tooling existed in legacy product (git, code search) but no first-class verbatim list on the surfaces reviewed.

topic-based-routing.note: Multi-prompt agents and Conversation Flow agents are state machines that route the live conversation between prompts/nodes by transition conditions.
Instructions define routing across skills; each playbook's LLM description tells the top-level agent or crew when to route into it.

typed-tool-loop-detector.note: Tool-loop detection is mechanical at the dispatch boundary with five typed failure modes (repeat, unknown, poll, ping-pong, circuit-breaker) and per-tool caps, returning a structured refusal the model must consume rather than a prompt-level reminder.

vector-memory.note: Archival memory is a semantically searchable vector DB that the agent queries on demand via tools when content cannot fit in core memory.
Extracted memories land in managed vector storage; search returns by semantic similarity.
Embedding-indexed memory sits alongside the Markdown corpus so semantically similar items can be retrieved from a query, not just keyword matches.

visual-workflow-graph.vendor: Baidu (百度) — Baidu Intelligent Cloud / Qianfan
visual-workflow-graph.note: Hosted Qianfan AppBuilder console exposes a visual workflow editor; SDK mirrors the same model in code through AgentRuntime over Components.
Workflow orchestration is the central abstraction; supports loops, parallelism, batch and conditional logic in one graph.
Studio is a visual, drag-and-drop interface for building agents; Workflows are graphs of Nodes connected by transitions.
Workflow editor is described in the Coze Studio README as a drag-and-drop visual canvas for assembling typed nodes into an executable business process.
Homepage and README both describe a visual workflow canvas with rich node types; the workflow IS the agent in FastGPT.
AgentFlow V2 explicitly frames itself as a paradigm shift toward 'explicit workflow orchestration' on a visual node canvas; Agentflow is documented as superset of Chatflow + Assistant.
Flows are explicitly 'functional representations of application workflows' composed of connected components on a visual canvas.
Stack AI's primary surface is a visual workflow builder; Core Nodes are 'the primary building blocks of every StackAI workflow.'
Workflows are explicitly a visual builder; playbooks and workflows are the two named primitives of the build surface.

world-model-separation.note: OASIS-style large-scale simulation distinguishes simulated environment from agent reasoning, but the framework does not document an explicit world-model API.
